title: A Grammarianן¿½s Funeral
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shortly after the Revival of Learning in Europe


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let us begin and carry up this corpse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing together.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leave we the common crofts, the vulgar thorpes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each in its tether
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sleeping safe on the bosom of the plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cared-for till cock-crow:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Look out if yonder be not day again
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rimming the rock-row!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s the appropriate country; there, manן¿½s thought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rarer, intenser,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Self-gathered for an outbreak, as it ought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Chafes in the censer.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leave we the unlettered plain its herd and crop;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seek we sepulture
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On a tall mountain, citied to the top,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crowded with culture!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All the peaks soar, but one the rest excels;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Clouds overcome it;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No! yonder sparkle is the citadelן¿½s
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Circling its summit.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thither our path lies; wind we up the heights:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wait ye the warning?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our low life was the levelן¿½s and the nightן¿½s;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s for the morning.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Step to a tune, square chests, erect each head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ware the beholders!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This is our master, famous, calm and dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Borne on our shoulders.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sleep, crop and herd! sleep, darkling thorpe and croft,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Safe from the weather!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He, whom we convoy to his grave aloft,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing together,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was a man born with thy face and throat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lyric Apollo!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Long he lived nameless: how should spring take note
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Winter would follow?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till lo, the little touch, and youth was gone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cramped and diminished,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Moaned he, ן¿½New measures, other feet anon!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My dance is finishedן¿½?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No, thatן¿½s the worldן¿½s way: (keep the mountain-side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Make for the city!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He knew the signal, and stepped on with pride
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Over menן¿½s pity;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Left play for work, and grappled with the world
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bent on escaping:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whatן¿½s in the scroll,ן¿½ quoth he, ן¿½thou keepest furled
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Show me their shaping,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theirs who most studied man, the bard and sage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give!ן¿½ So, he gowned him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Straight got by heart that book to its last page:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Learned, we found him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yea, but we found him bald too, eyes like lead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Accents uncertain:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Time to taste life,ן¿½ another would have said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Up with the curtain!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This man said rather, ן¿½Actual life comes next?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Patience a moment!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grant I have mastered learningן¿½s crabbed text,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still thereן¿½s the comment.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let me know all! Prate not of most or least,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Painful or easy!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Even to the crumbs Iן¿½d fain eat up the feast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ay, nor feel queasy.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, such a life as he resolved to live,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he had learned it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he had gathered all books had to give!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sooner, he spurned it.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Image the whole, then execute the parts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fancy the fabric
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quite, ere you build, ere steel strike fire from quartz,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere mortar dab brick!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Hereן¿½s the town-gate reached: thereן¿½s the market-place
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gaping before us.)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yea, this in him was the peculiar grace
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Hearten our chorus!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That before living heן¿½d learn how to live,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No end to learning:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Earn the means first, God surely will contrive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Use for our earning.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Others mistrust and say, ן¿½But time escapes:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Live now or never!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He said, ן¿½Whatן¿½s time? Leave Now for dogs and apes!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Man has Forever.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Back to his book then: deeper drooped his head:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Calculus racked him:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leaden before, his eyes grew dross of lead:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tussis attacked him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now, master, take a little rest!ן¿½ not he!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Caution redoubled
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Step two abreast, the way winds narrowly!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not a whit troubled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Back to his studies, fresher than at first,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fierce as a dragon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He (soul-hydroptic with a sacred thirst)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sucked at the flagon.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, if we draw a circle premature,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heedless of far gain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Greedy for quick returns of profit, sure
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bad is our bargain!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was it not great? did not he throw on God,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (He loves the burthen),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s task to make the heavenly period
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perfect the earthen?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did not he magnify the mind, show clear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just what it all meant?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He would not discount life, as fools do here,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Paid by instalment.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He ventured neck or nothing, heavenן¿½s success
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Found, or earthן¿½s failure:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Wilt thou trust death or not?ן¿½ He answered ן¿½Yes:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hence with lifeן¿½s pale lure!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That low man seeks a little thing to do,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sees it and does it:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This high man, with a great thing to pursue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dies ere he knows it.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That low man goes on adding one to one,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His hundredן¿½s soon hit:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This high man, aiming at a million,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Misses an unit.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That, has the world here, should he need the next,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let the world mind him!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This, throws himself on God, and unperplexed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seeking shall find him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, with the throttling hands of death at strife,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ground he at grammar;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still, throן¿½ the rattle, parts of speech were rife:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While he could stammer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He settled Hotiן¿½s business, let it be!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Properly based Oun,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gave us the doctrine of the enclitic De,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dead from the waist down.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, hereן¿½s the platform, hereן¿½s the proper place:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hail to your purlieus,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All ye highfliers of the feathered race,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Swallows and curlews!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hereן¿½s the top-peak; the multitude below
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Live, for they can, there:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This man decided not to Live but Know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bury this man there?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here, hereן¿½s his place, where meteors shoot, clouds form,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lightnings are loosened,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stars come and go! Let joy break with the storm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Peace let the dew send!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lofty designs must close in like effects:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loftily lying,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leave him, still loftier than the world suspects,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Living and dying.
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
